title: FCC to repeal 39% TV ownership cap in boost for Trump-friendly news orgs
gist: FCC Chairman Brendan Carr plans to repeal the 39% national TV ownership cap and replace it with discretionary case-by-case merger review, a move that favors Trump-friendly broadcasters like Nexstar and sets up a legal battle over whether the FCC (versus Congress) has authority to change a limit set in federal law.

themes.theme: FCC deregulation opens the door to station consolidation

insights.claim: FCC Chairman Brendan Carr plans to repeal the 39% national TV ownership cap at the commission's August 6, 2026 meeting and replace it with a discretionary case-by-case review of each proposed station merger, letting the FCC selectively decide which broadcasters may exceed the limit.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: A discretionary approval regime replaces a hard cap, which could unlock further station-group consolidation (and re-rate broadcaster M&A valuations) for companies favored by the sitting FCC chair.
insights.locator: The Federal Communications Commission will vote to repeal

insights.claim: In March 2026 the Carr-led FCC granted Nexstar Media Group a waiver to acquire Tegna, pushing Nexstar's household reach to 54.5% under the 'UHF discount' calculation (and 80% without it), which the FCC treated as a test case for waiving the 39% cap ahead of the formal repeal.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Establishes the real-world scale of ownership concentration already permitted under waivers, a template for future large-scale broadcast M&A once the formal cap is repealed.
insights.locator: FCC Chairman Brendan Carr has already treated the rule

themes.theme: Legal risk to the repeal and pending mergers

insights.claim: Commissioner Anna Gomez and outside legal groups (United Church of Christ Media Justice Ministry, Public Knowledge, Free Press, and the Communications Workers of America) argue only Congress can change the 39% cap, because Congress explicitly barred the FCC from granting waivers to it when it amended Section 202 of the Telecommunications Act of 1996.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The repeal is likely to face immediate legal challenges, creating downside/reversal risk for any station-group deals that rely on the case-by-case review process to exceed 39% reach.
insights.quote: Congress set the 39 percent national ownership cap in federal law, and only Congress has the authority to raise or eliminate it. The commission cannot waive away that limit simply because these corporate behemoths want to get out from under it.
insights.locator: Only Congress can change cap, critics say

insights.claim: Congress set the current 39% cap in 2004 specifically to reverse an FCC attempt to raise the limit to 45% in 2003, establishing a direct precedent that Congress has previously stepped in within months to overturn FCC action on this exact rule.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: History suggests Congress could again legislatively reverse an FCC repeal, adding regulatory-reversal risk for investors in broadcasters betting on the case-by-case review regime holding.
insights.locator: Gomez was referring to a 2004 vote

insights.claim: A federal judge has already ordered Nexstar and Tegna to halt integrating their assets and operations while an antitrust case brought by DirecTV proceeds, even though the Tegna acquisition legally closed on March 19, 2026; Nexstar is appealing.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Shows that FCC approval alone does not eliminate antitrust litigation risk to broadcast megadeals, a relevant precedent for any transaction structured under the new case-by-case regime.
insights.locator: FCC accused of illegally waiving limit for Nexstar

insights.claim: Carr frames the ownership cap repeal as pro-localism, arguing in a Breitbart op-ed that trust in 'legacy national media' is low (especially among Republicans) and that removing the cap will let station groups gain scale to invest in local reporting rather than acting as 'mouthpieces for programming produced in New York and Hollywood.'
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: This stated rationale signals the FCC will likely approve mergers framed as advancing 'localism, viewpoint diversity, and competition,' giving dealmakers a playbook for structuring FCC-friendly transactions.
insights.quote: Americans no longer trust the legacy national media to report the news fairly or accurately.
insights.locator: Carr now plans to repeal the 39 percent limit

insights.claim: Commissioner Gomez counters that removing the cap will 'destroy local newsrooms, silence community reporting, and drive up costs for the American families who depend on local stations for news and emergency alerts,' framing the repeal as enabling billionaire-backed consolidation rather than protecting localism.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Two opposing investment theses exist on how station-group valuations will re-rate: bullish (consolidation scale benefits) versus bearish (regulatory/legal backlash, service-quality erosion) — both plausible depending on litigation outcomes.
insights.quote: unlawful effort to hand control of the public airwaves to billionaire buddies of this administration

insights.claim: Nexstar and the National Association of Broadcasters, the primary broadcaster lobby, support eliminating the cap, arguing broadcasters are uniquely restricted compared to digital competitors, since 'no one would suggest limiting the reach of YouTube, Amazon, or CNN.'
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Industry lobbying using a 'digital competitor' framing could extend beyond TV ownership caps to further cross-ownership deregulation (e.g., broadcast-cable or broadcast-digital combinations) worth watching for future FCC rulemakings.
insights.quote: no one would suggest limiting the reach of YouTube, Amazon, or CNN, yet local broadcasters are still forced to compete under rules written for a different century.
